one_line_takeaway: A taxonomy-expanding, raw-text-conditioned synthetic pipeline that generates 51k diverse jailbreak prompts and DPO alignment triplets, jailbreaking 10 LLMs at 100% macro-category ASR — though the metric is lenient and the safety-improvement claim is left to future work.
key_finding: SAGE-generated prompts achieved 100% ASR at the macro-category level for all 10 evaluated models (i.e., every model was jailbroken in at least one prompt per macro-category). At sub-category granularity, GPT-4o, GPT-3.5-turbo, Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.1, and gemma-7b-it hit 100%, while the hardest model, Llama-3-8B-Instruct, still fell in 90.63% of sub-categories. Leaf-category ASR (the strictest, over 279 leaf categories) varied widely: from 20.79% (Llama-3-8B-Instruct, the safest) up to 91.04% (Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.1). Coding-based and Story-based prompts were the most effective jailbreak types. The authors conclude raw-text-conditioned expansion yields more diverse/nuanced attacks than taxonomy-only methods.
core_contribution: SAGE-RT (SAGE) is a three-stage pipeline that generates synthetic red-teaming and safety-alignment data for LLMs starting only from a harmfulness taxonomy, without requiring large manually-curated seed datasets. Its distinguishing move over prior synthetic-data work (AART, TAP/PAIR, Wild-teaming, Rainbow-teaming) is that it first algorithmically EXPANDS the ALERT taxonomy into fine-grained 'leaf' (sub-sub) categories, then generates toxic raw-text (blogs, articles, book summaries, social posts) per leaf topic and extracts queries CONDITIONED on that raw-text. This raw-text conditioning is claimed to surface niche sub-tasks and constrained variants of a harmful task (e.g., not just 'bomb making' but 'procuring bomb-grade ammonium nitrate' or 'how can a 23-year-old with $40 build a bomb?') that taxonomy-only methods miss, while avoiding mode collapse. The authors produced 51,000 prompt-response pairs spanning ~1,500 topics and 320 leaf categories, and convert red-team prompts into DPO preference triplets (query, rejected/safe response, accepted/toxic response) for downstream alignment training.
threat_model: Adversary is a red-teamer (or attacker) seeking to jailbreak safety-aligned LLMs into producing harmful/unsafe content across a broad, nuanced range of harm categories. Attack surface is black-box prompting of the target LLM. The paper frames SAGE both defensively (generate diverse alignment/DPO data to make models safer) and offensively (systematically probe model vulnerabilities). Attacks are single-turn jailbreak prompts of 9 pre-defined types: direct question, biased, toxic-sentence-completion, fictional scenario, roleplaying, story writing, coding task, sub-task-based, and constrained-situation.
limitations: (1) No empirical head-to-head benchmark against the cited baselines (AART, TAP, PAIR, Wild-teaming, Rainbow-teaming) — their weaknesses (mode collapse, cost, lack of nuance) are argued qualitatively, not measured. (2) The headline '100% ASR' relies on a very lenient metric (one successful prompt per category), which the paper itself warns 'DOES NOT mean all prompts were successful'; per-prompt success rates are only in appendix Table 3. (3) The defensive claim is unvalidated: no experiment shows that training on SAGE's DPO/alignment data actually improves model safety — DPO/ORPO/SimPO results are deferred to 'future work'. (4) Single automated judge (GPT-4o) with no human validation or false-positive analysis of jailbreak labeling. (5) Diversity is shown only via embedding-cluster/UMAP and N-gram visualizations, not a rigorous diversity metric or human audit. (6) Dataset quality depends heavily on one uncensored generator (SolarLM); generalization of the pipeline to other harm taxonomies is asserted but not demonstrated.

datasets_used: ALERT harmfulness taxonomy (Tedeschi et al. 2024) — 6 macro-categories, 32 sub-categories; used as the seed taxonomy
SAGE-generated dataset: 51,000 prompt-response pairs, ~1,500 topics, 320 generated leaf (sub-sub) categories; 279 leaf-categories evaluated
Generator models used to build data: Mistral / Mixtral-8x7B (instructions + query extraction), SolarLM 10.7B (uncensored/toxic raw-text and toxic responses), Llama-3-instruct (safe/aligned responses)
Cited (not used) prior datasets: Nvidia/AEGIS AI Safety, Anthropic RLHF (Bai et al. 2022), LMSYS-1M, Do-Not-Answer
quantitative_results: Macro-category ASR = 100.0% for all 10 evaluated models

Sub-category ASR: Llama-3-8B-Instruct 90.63, Llama-3-70B-Instruct 96.88, Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.1 100.0, gpt-4-0125-preview 84.38, gpt-4o 100.0, gpt-3.5-turbo 100.0, gpt-4-turbo-2024-04-09 96.88, Llama-2-7b-chat-hf 90.63, Llama-2-70b-chat-hf 96.88, gemma-7b-it 100.0

Leaf-category ASR (279 leaf-cats): Llama-3-8B-Instruct 20.79, Llama-3-70B-Instruct 46.95, Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.1 91.04, gpt-4-0125-preview 25.09, gpt-4o 63.08, gpt-3.5-turbo 74.05, gpt-4-turbo-2024-04-09 31.9, Llama-2-7b-chat-hf 29.03, Llama-2-70b-chat-hf 29.03, gemma-7b-it 59.50
Safest model Llama-3-8B-Instruct still vulnerable in 55 of 279 leaf-categories (~20.79%)
Jailbreaks succeeded in >27 of 32 sub-categories and >58 of 279 leaf-categories across SOTA models
Evaluation scale: 500 prompts sampled per macro-category x 6 = 3,000 prompts per model; 9 jailbreak prompt types per raw-text
Most effective jailbreak types: Coding-based and Story-based prompts
baselines_compared: AART (Radharapu et al. 2023) — qualitative comparison only (criticized for mode collapse, repetitive prefixes, lack of nuance)
TAP / Tree-of-Attacks-Pruning (Mehrotra et al. 2023) — qualitative (slow, mode collapse, poor scaling with #goals)
PAIR (Chao et al. 2023) — qualitative (limited diversity, poor scaling)
Wild-teaming (Jiang et al. 2024b) — qualitative (needs manual LMSYS-1M seed, misses nuanced sub-tasks)
Rainbow-teaming (Samvelyan et al. 2024) — qualitative (goals limited to taxonomy mixes)
NOTE: no quantitative/empirical head-to-head ASR comparison against any of these baselines is reported
